# Raw research: Content repurposing, hour budgets, 70/30, SEO/GEO, homiletics (Prompt 13)

**Generated:** 2026-04-12  
**Methodology:** Web retrieval of primary pages (Orbit Media annual blogger survey; arXiv GEO paper; Google Search Central help; CMI topical hub; industry reports where full PDF not ingested). Cross-read with internal [00-existing-corpus-audit.md](./00-existing-corpus-audit.md) and [../articles/11-voice-preservation.md](../articles/11-voice-preservation.md) (70/30 + disclosure). This file stress-tests Movemental claims keyed as `C-F05`, `research-025`, `research-029`, `articles-039`, `business-082`, plus the “48 sermons → 48 artifacts” illustration.

---

## 1. Repurposing in content marketing (industry + strategy)

### 1.1 Popular frameworks (verify, do not treat as empirical law)

- **“Content pyramid / pillar + splinters”** — Gary Vaynerchuk and adjacent influencer literature popularized aggressive atomization of one core asset into dozens of derivative clips. Treat as **distribution heuristic**, not a controlled study of quality or conversion.
- **CMI operational guidance** — Content Marketing Institute publishes remix/recycle/repurpose playbooks (e.g. [Remix, recycle, repurpose](https://contentmarketinginstitute.com/content-operations/remix-recycle-repurpose), accessed 2026-04-12). Emphasis: **plan modular “Lego” assets**, evergreen topics, cross-team handoffs. This is **process wisdom**, not a randomized trial of “repurposed vs original” ranking lift.

### 1.2 Empirical performance: what we can and cannot claim

- **Blogging benchmarks (time, length, AI use)** — Orbit Media, *Blogging Statistics* (12th annual survey, 2025), self-reported by **n = 808** marketers; reports **~21%** “strong results” as benchmark; average article **1,333 words**; average time per article **“just under three and a half hours”** (context on page: time per piece has **declined in recent years**, “possibly because of AI”). Source: [orbitmedia.com/blog/blogging-statistics/](https://www.orbitmedia.com/blog/blogging-statistics/) (retrieved 2026-04-12).
- **AI use correlation** — Same Orbit 2025 article: **“just one in ten use AI to write complete articles”**; cohort using AI to write **complete** articles is **least likely** to report “strong results” (wording on page; interpret as **negative correlation in their sample**, not causal proof). Marketers using AI **as an editor** are described as **as likely** to report strong results as those using human editors (per page narrative—**self-report**, same survey limitations).
- **Takeaway for Movemental** — Industry data supports **“repurposing + editorial assistance can be part of high-performing programs”** but **does not** license a claim that **fully AI-drafted** repurposed assets match human-led editorial outcomes.

### 1.3 Religious-leader / sermon-scale repurposing (evidence gap)

- **Peer-reviewed “sermon → article conversion rate”** — Not located in this pass. Most available material is **vendor case studies** (transcription SaaS, church marketing agencies) or **journalism** (clergy + AI ethics). **Flag as major gap** rather than inventing statistics.
- **Adjacent signal (clergy + AI writing)** — NPR (Jul 17, 2025) summarizes Barna-reported split: **12%** of Protestant clergy comfortable using AI to **write** sermons vs **43%** seeing merit for **research/prep** ([npr.org/2025/07/17/nx-s1-5468637/…](https://www.npr.org/2025/07/17/nx-s1-5468637/clergy-grapple-with-the-ethics-of-using-ai-to-write-sermons), retrieved 2026-04-12). Useful for **positioning** Movemental toward **assistive** workflows, not “ghostwriter for Sunday.”

---

## 2. The “five-hour week” platform maintenance claim (`research-025`)

### 2.1 Benchmark: single-channel writing time already approaches the budget

- Orbit Media (2025): **~3.5 hours per blog article** alone, before email, social, course updates, community moderation, video, or legal/metadata work ([same URL](https://www.orbitmedia.com/blog/blogging-statistics/)).
- **Apples-to-oranges risk** — Movemental’s “platform” stack (articles + courses + email + social + AI Lab) is **multi-channel**. Summing even **modest** per-channel times typically **exceeds 5 hours/week** unless scope is aggressively capped (frequency, format depth, or delegated staff).

### 2.2 Creator burnout (cadence pressure)

- ConvertKit / Kit and adjacent summaries on **creator burnout** and **multi-platform pressure** appear repeatedly in **State of the Creator Economy** reporting (e.g. Kit partner report hub: [partners.convertkit.com/2024report](https://partners.convertkit.com/2024report), landing page retrieved 2026-04-12; secondary writeups cite **~59%** burnout among full-time creators—**verify inside PDF** before hard marketing use).
- Buffer resources document burnout syndromes tied to **always-on publishing** ([buffer.com/resources/creator-burnout/](https://buffer.com/resources/creator-burnout/), retrieved 2026-04-12).

### 2.3 AI time-saving claims (vendor-heavy)

- CoSchedule help-center style pages (2025–2026) claim large majorities of AI-using marketers report **speed** gains; specific **“5+ hours/week”** figures appear in marketing summaries—**treat as vendor-collected until methodology PDF reviewed** ([help.coschedule.com/ai-marketing-statistics-2025](http://help.coschedule.com/ai-marketing-statistics-2025), retrieved 2026-04-12).

### 2.4 Hidden workflow hours (prompt Q16)

Typical unstated buckets for “sermon → public article” pipelines:

- **Capture:** recording quality, file handling, consent.  
- **Transcription + diarization + cleanup.**  
- **Theological / factual QA** (quotes, Scripture references, contextualized anecdotes).  
- **Structural rewrite** (oral → written genre).  
- **Rights** (music, guest stories, identifiable third parties).  
- **CMS, metadata, OG images, internal links.**  
- **Distribution + reply handling.**

Surveys that only ask “how long to write this post?” **undercount** these.

---

## 3. The 70/30 rule (`research-029`, overlaps `business-032` / Prompt 11)

### 3.1 Research status

- **No** widely accepted empirical optimum for “70% AI / 30% human” across domains. Internal article [11-voice-preservation.md](../articles/11-voice-preservation.md) already classifies 70/30 as **operations design**, not universal law, citing motivation/metacognition concerns (e.g. `doi:10.1038/s41598-025-98385-2`, `doi:10.1111/bjet.13544`).

### 3.2 Productivity-oriented academic signals (context: graduate / academic writing, not sermons)

- Field-experiment and RCT-style papers on **GPT-assisted** drafting/report writing report **measurable time savings** and sometimes **clarity** gains on specific rubric dimensions; caveats include **originality**, **rigor**, and **overconfidence**. Examples surfaced in secondary summaries: Mannheim MZES publication page on GPT-4 field experiment with early-career social scientists ([mzes.uni-mannheim.de/en/publications/details/…](https://www.mzes.uni-mannheim.de/en/publications/details/harnessing-gpt-for-enhanced-academic-writing-evidence-from-a-field-experiment-with-early-career-researchers-in-the-social-sciences), retrieved 2026-04-12); SSRN-style working papers on graduate writing ([papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4941022](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4941022), retrieved 2026-04-12—**verify peer-review status** before treating as settled).

### 3.3 Reader detection / trust

- Pew (2025) experiments on **post-hoc disclosure** of AI involvement in **political speeches** and **news** show substantial negative reactions ([pewresearch.org/short-reads/2025/09/17/…](https://www.pewresearch.org/short-reads/2025/09/17/from-political-speeches-to-songs-how-would-americans-react-if-they-found-out-ai-was-involved/), retrieved 2026-04-12). Movemental should assume **religious teaching content** skews toward high-stakes disclosure norms (closer to speech/news than to entertainment memes).

---

## 4. SEO: duplication, canonicalization, thin content, E-E-A-T

### 4.1 Duplicate URLs / near-duplicates

- Google Search Central documents **canonicalization** (`rel=canonical`, redirects, sitemaps) as the primary control surface when similar URLs exist ([developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls](https://developers.google.com/search/docs/crawling-indexing/consolidate-duplicate-urls), retrieved 2026-04-12). Google’s public guidance frames duplicate clusters as **normal** on the web; systems attempt to pick a **canonical** URL for ranking—**not** the same as “any repurposing is safe for visibility.”

### 4.2 Thin / low-value syndication

- Separate risk: **thin** pages that add little beyond duplicated transcript blocks can struggle on **quality** grounds independent of “duplicate content penalty” folklore. Pair with **E-E-A-T** heuristics (experience, expertise, authoritativeness, trust) already internalized in Movemental corpus (see Prompt 00 bibliography).

---

## 5. GEO / AI-mediated discovery (`business-082` adjacent)

- Aggarwal et al., **GEO: Generative Engine Optimization**, arXiv:2311.09735v3 (rev. 2024-06-28), frames generative engines as **multi-source synthesizers** and proposes optimization strategies; reports **up to ~40%** relative visibility improvements **in their benchmark** (not a guarantee for any single pastor site). URL: [arxiv.org/abs/2311.09735](https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.09735).

**Implication for “sermon → article”** — Long-form may still matter for **citations** and **owned narrative**, but **discovery** increasingly passes through **models that compress many sources**. Movemental messaging should avoid implying **classic SEO alone** equals future **LLM visibility**.

---

## 6. Homiletics: spoken word vs written essay (prompt Q12)

### 6.1 Standard references (books, not web stats)

- **Fred B. Craddock**, *As One Without Authority: Essays on Inductive Preaching* (1971; later editions)—inductive movement and listener-centered form; archive of sermon materials at Fred Craddock Center ([fredcraddockcenter.org](https://fredcraddockcenter.org/craddock-archive/), retrieved 2026-04-12).
- **Thomas G. Long**, *The Witness of Preaching* (standard homiletics textbook across editions)—treats preaching as **oral witness** with distinct rhetorical obligations vs print argumentation.
- **Barbara Brown Taylor** — influential on **embodied, situational** preaching; not a license for lazy reuse, but for **contextual integrity** when adapting speech to text.

### 6.2 Practical homiletical tension

- Moving sermon → polished article is not **neutral formatting**. It is often **re-authorship**: removing redundancy, fixing oral transitions, tightening logic, anonymizing stories, adding references—**editorial**, not clerical.

---

## 7. Failure modes when scaling repurposing without standards (prompt Q18)

Documented patterns (mixed sources: content marketing commentary + AI-slop discourse + pastoral ethics):

- **Quality collapse** — templated “AI slurry,” repeated hot takes, lost local/contextual grounding.  
- **Audience fatigue** — same anecdote across email, blog, podcast, and social in one week.  
- **Brand dilution** — voice drifts as model temperature + prompt churn accumulate.  
- **Doctrinal / pastoral misalignment** — confident hallucinations or flattened nuance (see Prompt 11 “almost-right” failure mode).

---

## 8. Confidence table (Movemental-facing claims)

| Claim ID / phrase | Confidence | One-sentence rationale |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `C-F05` sermon-to-article “capture and transform” as **legitimate workflow** | **Verified** (as workflow, not as automatic quality) | Homiletics + publishing practice both accept adaptation of spoken teaching to print when ethically edited; clergy surveys favor **assistive** AI over ghostwriting. |
| `research-025` ~**5 hours/week** sustains full multi-channel platform | **Unverified / likely over-broad** as stated | Single-article industry means alone approach **half** that budget; hidden pipeline hours and multi-channel cadence usually dominate. |
| `research-029` **70/30** as optimal ratio | **Contradicted** as empirical law | No robust cross-domain optimum; internal Prompt 11 already reframes as governance design; Orbit 2025 suggests **full AI drafting** correlates with **weaker** self-reported results. |
| `articles-039` repurposing as **necessary** in fragmented attention economy | **Partially supported** | Strategic case strong (CMI, creator-economy cadence pressures); **moral “necessary”** is rhetorical, not statistical. |
| “48 sermons → 48 articles + guides + social series” | **Partially supported** as **illustration**, not arithmetic law | Feasible as **SKU count** with team tooling; **misleading** if implied **zero marginal editorial** time per derivative. |
| `business-082` pipeline demo implies **end-to-end low labor** | **Unverified** until bounded with **scope** (what automation covers, what human must sign) | Product demos risk cherry-picking easiest transcripts + senior editors off-camera. |

---

## 9. Counter-arguments (use in honest marketing)

1. **Orbit 2025:** marketers who use AI to write **entire** articles are **least likely** to report “strong results” — cuts against “AI does most, human sprinkles” as a results strategy.  
2. **Pew 2025:** post-hoc AI disclosure can **damage trust** even when people liked the content—cuts against hiding assistance.  
3. **Clergy split (NPR/Barna):** discomfort with AI **writing** sermons—Movemental must distinguish **study/prep/drafting** from **Sunday proclamation**.  
4. **GEO:** generative engines may **summarize away** brand-specific phrasing even when articles exist—**visibility ≠ traffic** in legacy sense.

---

## 10. Suggested metrics for first cohorts (replace narrative with data)

- **Editorial minutes per published artifact** (by type: article, guide, social batch).  
- **Human edit delta** (insertions/deletions vs first automated draft).  
- **Time-to-publish** from raw recording → live URL.  
- **Canonical URL policy** compliance rate.  
- **Search + AI referral** breakdown (GSC + emerging analytics).  
- **Pastoral incident log** (factual errors, misquotes, pastoral harm) per 100 publishes.

---
